This neuroinflammation, superimposed on a vulnerable brain - the aged, the patient with pre-existing cognitive impairment, or with other risk factors - is believed to contribute to delirium in the early postoperative period and to the more prolonged cognitive decline some patients experience. Other elements of the stress response may contribute: the hypercortisolaemia, sleep disruption, pain, and the metabolic disturbances. The implication is that measures attenuating the stress and inflammatory response, together with good analgesia, avoidance of deliriogenic drugs, maintenance of physiology and early mobilisation, may reduce these complications, which is an active area of research. So the cytokine-driven neuroinflammation of the stress response is thought to contribute to postoperative delirium and cognitive dysfunction, especially in the vulnerable elderly brain, providing a further rationale for attenuating the inflammatory response and optimising perioperative care.

qa.q: Briefly, what is the proposed link between the stress response, immunosuppression and cancer surgery?
qa.a: An intriguing and clinically important hypothesis links the stress and inflammatory response of cancer surgery to the bodys ability to control residual tumour, and although unproven it has driven considerable research into anaesthetic technique. The reasoning is as follows. Surgery to remove a cancer inevitably risks shedding tumour cells into the circulation and leaving micrometastatic disease, and the bodys defence against these residual cells depends substantially on cell-mediated immunity, particularly the natural killer cells and cytotoxic T cells. The surgical stress response, however, produces a period of immunosuppression - through the immunomodulatory effects of cortisol and catecholamines, the compensatory anti-inflammatory phase following the cytokine surge, and the effects of pain, hypothermia and transfusion - that depresses precisely this cell-mediated, natural-killer-cell immunity at the very time it is needed to eliminate residual cancer cells. The hypothesis therefore proposes that a larger stress response and greater perioperative immunosuppression might permit more tumour to survive and so increase the risk of recurrence, and conversely that attenuating the response - for instance with regional anaesthesia, opioid-sparing techniques and good analgesia - might preserve immunity and improve cancer outcomes. This is biologically plausible and supported by some laboratory and observational data, but large randomised trials have so far not confirmed a clear benefit of one technique over another, so it remains an unproven hypothesis. So the proposed link is that the stress-induced immunosuppression of cancer surgery may impair the cell-mediated defence against residual tumour, raising the theoretical possibility that attenuating the response improves cancer outcomes - a plausible but as yet unproven hypothesis.

qa.q: Thank you. An excellent and complete account. Let us stop there. Before we close, here are the take-home messages. First, The stress response is the integrated neuroendocrine (HPA axis and cortisol, sympathoadrenal catecholamines, raised vasopressin, aldosterone, glucagon, growth hormone; suppressed insulin), metabolic (catabolic, hyperglycaemic with insulin resistance, protein and fat breakdown, salt and water retention) and inflammatory (cytokine, acute-phase) reaction to surgical trauma. Second, It is driven chiefly by nociceptive afferent neural traffic from the wound plus humoral cytokines (notably interleukin-6), and its magnitude and duration are proportional to the severity of surgery. Third, Protective in evolutionary origin but largely redundant and harmful in excess in the supported patient - causing muscle wasting, impaired healing, hyperglycaemia, immunosuppression, thromboembolism and cardiac stress - so attenuating it is a goal of modern care. Fourth, Regional and neuraxial anaesthesia is the most effective means of attenuation (blocking the afferent drive, best for lower-body surgery and the neuroendocrine arm), supported by opioids, adequate depth, multimodal analgesia, minimal-access surgery, normothermia, euvolaemia, carbohydrate loading and early nutrition and mobilisation - the substance of enhanced recovery. Fifth, Patients on long-term steroids or with adrenal insufficiency cannot mount the cortisol response and need perioperative steroid cover graded to surgical severity; control perioperative glucose and give thromboprophylaxis. Thank you. That concludes the station.
